definition: The private sector must absorb ~$2.0-2.6T/year in net Treasury duration (new issuance + QT runoff) vs a pre-pandemic norm of $500-800B. The marginal buyer has shifted from price-insensitive (central banks, foreign official) to price-sensitive (hedge funds via basis trades, asset managers, households). This combination may be super-additive: removing the price-insensitive buyer increases auction volatility and required risk premium in a convex manner.
evidence: Net Treasury duration absorption: ~$2.0-2.6T/year vs pre-pandemic $500-800B
Marginal buyer shift from price-insensitive to price-sensitive
Weak auction tails in late 2023 through 2025 provide suggestive evidence
GDP-normalization reduces the multiplier from 4-5x to ~2.5x (challenger_02 refinement)
New-issue concessions widened to 15-25bp vs. near-zero during QE
Pre-QE markets routinely absorbed all sovereign issuance at 4-5% yields
Duration supply = fiscal deficit = private sector income (double-entry accounting identity)
open_questions: Is the interaction truly super-additive or merely additive?
Will auction volatility increase structurally as price-sensitive buyers dominate?
Does the QT term premium effect compound with or substitute for fiscal supply effects?
At what term premium level do price-insensitive buyers (insurers, pensions with ALM mandates) return as marginal buyers?
Is the compound absorption problem about pricing (term premium) or solvency?
Does the June 2024 QT taper provide a natural experiment for decomposing effects?
